sentences.base_language: Akari had made a reservation at a new restaurant in the city that everyone had been talking about.
sentences.target_language: あかりは、街で話題になっていた新しいレストランを予約していた。
sentences.romanized: Akari wa, machi de wadai ni natte ita atarashii resutoran wo yoyaku shite ita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ていた past progressive / resultant state**: 話題になっ**ていた** (had been the talk of the town — an ongoing state up to this point). **〜ていた** describes a state or process that was in progress before a narrative reference point, setting the background scene. Here it conveys that the restaurant's reputation had already been building. **予約していた** similarly uses **〜ていた** to show the reservation existed as a completed, held state going into the evening.
**〜になる change of state**: 話題**になる** (become a topic / become talked about). **〜になる** marks a transition into a new state: 有名**になる** (become famous), 心配**になる** (become worried). The phrase **話題になる** is a fixed collocation meaning 'to become the talk of the town / to be widely discussed': あの映画が**話題になっている** (that film is the talk of the town right now).

sentences.base_language: When they arrived, the restaurant was tastefully decorated and already nearly full of diners.
sentences.target_language: 着いてみると、レストランはセンスよく飾られていて、すでに客でほぼいっぱいだった。
sentences.romanized: Tsuite miru to, resutoran wa sensu yoku kazarare ite, sude ni kyaku de hobo ippai datta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜てみると discovery**: 着い**てみると** (when they arrived and looked — discovery on arrival). **〜てみると** combines the experiential **〜てみる** (to do and see) with the natural-result conditional **〜と**, producing a pattern of discovery: arriving and finding an unexpected or notable state. Compare plain **着くと** (when they arrived, as a natural result) — **〜てみると** adds the sense of what was observed upon seeing.
**Passive 〜られる with 〜ている**: 飾ら**れていて** (was decorated — passive resultant state). The passive **〜られる** combined with **〜ている** describes a state resulting from a prior action: 壁に絵が飾ら**れている** (paintings are hung on the wall), 丁寧に作ら**れている** (is carefully made). The te-form **〜ていて** then connects to the next clause, chaining descriptions.

sentences.base_language: A polite waiter greeted them at the entrance and showed them to a wonderful table by the window.
sentences.target_language: 丁寧なウェイターが入り口で出迎えてくれて、窓際の素晴らしいテーブルに案内してくれた。
sentences.romanized: Teinei na weitā ga iriguchi de demukae te kurete, madogiwa no subarashii tēburu ni annai shite kureta.
literal: 素晴らしいテーブルに
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜てくれる benefactive**: 出迎え**てくれて**、案内し**てくれた** (greeted them and showed them — doing these things as a kindness for Akari and her friend). **〜てくれる** marks that the action is performed by someone for the speaker's in-group, encoding appreciation. Chaining two **〜てくれる** actions with te-form emphasises that both courtesies were received in sequence.
**出迎える vs 迎える**: 出迎え**る** (demukae-ru) = to go out to greet, to meet someone on arrival — **出る** (go out) + **迎える** (receive/welcome). The **出** prefix implies physically going to the entrance to receive the guest, a mark of attentive service. **案内する** (annai suru) = to guide, to usher, to show someone around — a common keigo-adjacent collocation used in service contexts.

sentences.base_language: Akari felt immediately at ease — the atmosphere was elegant but not at all stiff or intimidating.
sentences.target_language: あかりはすぐにくつろいだ――雰囲気は上品だったが、まったく堅苦しくも威圧的でもなかった。
sentences.romanized: Akari wa sugu ni kutsuroida — fun'iki wa jōhin datta ga, mattaku katakurushiku mo iatsuteki de mo nakatta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜も〜も〜ない double negative**: 堅苦しく**も** 威圧的で**も** なかった (was neither stiff nor intimidating). Stacking **〜も** before a negative verb lists multiple things that are all equally absent: 時間**も** お金**も** ない (have neither time nor money). This is more emphatic than a single negation and efficiently dismisses both qualities at once.
**くつろぐ** (kutsurogu) = to relax, to feel at ease — an intransitive verb describing a comfortable, unhurried state: **くつろいだ** 雰囲気 (a relaxed atmosphere), **くつろいで** ください (please make yourself at home). **堅苦しい** (katakurushii) = stiff, formal to the point of discomfort, stuffy — an i-adjective: **堅苦しい** 場 (a stiff, formal setting). Its adverbial form **堅苦しく** is used here before **も〜ない**.

sentences.base_language: The menu was extensive, with carefully crafted dishes using seasonal Japanese ingredients.
sentences.target_language: メニューは充実していて、旬の国産食材を使った丁寧に作られた料理が並んでいた。
sentences.romanized: Menyū wa jūjitsu shite ite, shun no kokusan shokuzai wo tsukatta teinei ni tsukurarета ryōri ga narande ita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Stacked prenominal modifiers**: 旬の国産食材を使った / 丁寧に作られた / 料理 (dishes that used seasonal domestic ingredients and were carefully crafted). Japanese stacks relative clauses before the noun, each modifying the same head noun **料理**. There is no relative pronoun; the plain or past verb form directly precedes the noun it modifies. Stacking is natural and common in written and formal registers.
**充実する** (jūjitsu suru) = to be well-stocked, rich in content, substantial — often used of menus, curricula, or facilities: **充実した** プログラム (a rich programme), **充実している** (is fully equipped/extensive). **並ぶ** (narabu) = to line up, to be arranged — **料理が並んでいた** vividly pictures the dishes displayed or listed in an orderly array.

sentences.base_language: She decided on the chef's course menu and asked the waiter for a sake recommendation.
sentences.target_language: あかりはシェフのおまかせコースに決めて、ウェイターに日本酒のおすすめを聞いた。
sentences.romanized: Akari wa shefu no omakase kōsu ni kimete, weitā ni nihonshu no osusume wo kiita.
literal: シェフのおまかせコースに
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜に決める** (decide on ~): おまかせコース**に決める** (decide on the omakase course). **〜に決める** takes the chosen option marked with **に**, indicating the target of the decision: 東京**に決めた** (decided on Tokyo), Aプラン**に決めた** (went with Plan A). This is distinct from **〜することにする** (decide to do ~), which takes a verb clause.
**おまかせ** (omakase) = leaving the choice to the chef — from **任せる** (makaseru, to entrust/leave to someone). **おまかせコース** is a set course where the chef selects all dishes based on the season and availability — a mark of trust in the chef's expertise. **おすすめ** (o-susume) = recommendation — the honorific **お** prefix adds politeness: **おすすめ料理** (recommended dish), **おすすめはこちらです** (the recommendation is this).

sentences.base_language: He suggested a local sake from the Niigata region that he said would complement the seafood dishes beautifully.
sentences.target_language: ウェイターは、魚介料理によく合うと言って、新潟の地酒をすすめてくれた。
sentences.romanized: Weitā wa, gyokkai ryōri ni yoku au to itte, Niigata no jizake wo susumete kureta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜と言って** (quotative te-form): よく合う**と言って** すすめてくれた (recommended it, saying it would pair well). **〜と言って** embeds reported speech or a stated reason within a chain of actions. The speaker reports the waiter's claim (**よく合う** = pairs well) and then the action that followed (**すすめてくれた** = recommended). This pattern neatly combines indirect speech with narrative action.
**〜に合う** (to suit, to go well with): 魚介料理**に合う** (pairs well with seafood). **〜に合う** is a fixed collocation for pairing and suitability: ワインに**合う** 料理 (dishes that go with wine), 服に**合う** 靴 (shoes that match the outfit). **地酒** (jizake) = locally-produced sake, regional sake — **地** (ji, local/ground) + **酒** (sake): a term of pride for sake made in its home region.

sentences.base_language: The first course arrived — a delicate arrangement of seasonal sashimi with fresh garnish.
sentences.target_language: 最初の一皿が運ばれてきた――旬の刺身が新鮮なあしらいとともに繊細に盛り付けられていた。
sentences.romanized: Saisho no hito-sara ga hakobate kita — shun no sashimi ga shinsen na ashirai to tomo ni sensai ni moritsuke rarete ita.
literal: 新鮮なあしらいとともに
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜られてくる passive + directional**: 運ば**れてきた** (was brought — brought toward them). Combining passive **〜られる** with the directional auxiliary **〜てくる** describes something being brought to the speaker's location: 荷物が届け**られてきた** (the parcel was delivered here). **〜てくる** marks the movement as arriving at the speaker's frame, giving a vivid sense of the dish appearing before them.
**盛り付ける** (moritsuke-ru) = to plate, to arrange food on a dish — a culinary verb: **盛り付け** (the plating/presentation). The passive **盛り付けられていた** (had been plated) uses **〜ていた** to describe the resulting presentational state of the dish. **あしらい** = garnish, decorative accompaniment — a culinary term for the small elements that complete a dish's appearance and flavour balance.

sentences.base_language: Akari took her first bite and felt a genuine wave of delight — it was one of the finest things she had ever eaten.
sentences.target_language: あかりが一口食べてみると、本当の喜びの波が押し寄せてきた――これほどおいしいものを食べたことは、今までなかったかもしれない。
sentences.romanized: Akari ga hito-kuchi tabete miru to, hontō no yorokobi no nami ga oshiyosete kita — kore hodo oishii mono wo tabeta koto wa, ima made nakatta kamo shirenai.
literal: 今までなかったかもしれない。
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ほど degree marker**: これ**ほど** おいしいもの (something this delicious / delicious to this degree). **〜ほど** marks extent or degree by pointing to a reference: これ**ほど** (to this degree), あれ**ほど** 練習したのに (even though I practised that much). Combined with a negative or superlative context it creates a strong impression: こんなに**ほど** is colloquial; **これほど** is more literary and emphatic.
**〜たことがない** (have never done ~): 食べた**ことは…なかった** (had never eaten). **〜たことがある/ない** records whether an experience has occurred: 行った**ことがある** (have been there), 見た**ことがない** (have never seen). **かもしれない** (kamo shirenai) softens the assertion to 'perhaps / might be': 来ない**かもしれない** (might not come). The combination **なかったかもしれない** = 'had perhaps never' — a thoughtful, wondering tone rather than an absolute claim.

sentences.base_language: The main course was a slow-cooked wagyu dish with root vegetables and a rich dashi-based sauce.
sentences.target_language: メインは、根菜と濃厚な出汁ベースのソースを合わせた、じっくり煮込まれた和牛料理だった。
sentences.romanized: Mein wa, konsai to nōkō na dashi bēsu no sōsu wo awaseta, jikkuri nikomareta wagyū ryōri datta.
literal: 濃厚な出汁ベースのソースを
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜を合わせる** fixed collocation: ソースを**合わせる** (to combine / to pair a sauce). **〜を合わせる** is a culinary and general collocation for combining or pairing elements: 素材を**合わせる** (combine ingredients), 味を**合わせる** (balance the flavour). The past modifier 合わせ**た** here forms a prenominal clause describing the dish.
**じっくり** = slowly and carefully, taking time — a manner adverb used for processes that should not be rushed: **じっくり** 考える (think carefully), **じっくり** 煮込む (simmer slowly). **煮込む** (nikomu) = to stew, to simmer thoroughly — **煮る** (to boil/cook) + **込む** (to do deeply/thoroughly). The passive 煮込まれ**た** describes the wagyu as having undergone this slow process.

sentences.base_language: As they ate, Akari and her friend talked and laughed freely, completely losing track of time.
sentences.target_language: 食べながら、あかりと友人は自由に話し、笑い合って、すっかり時間を忘れてしまった。
sentences.romanized: Tabe nagara, Akari to yūjin wa jiyū ni hanashi, warai atte, sukkari jikan wo wasurete shimatta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ながら simultaneous action**: 食べ**ながら** 話した (talked while eating). **〜ながら** attaches to the masu-stem and expresses two concurrent actions by the same subject — the **〜ながら** action is the background; the main verb carries primary focus. Here eating is the backdrop against which conversation unfolds.
**〜合う reciprocal suffix**: 笑い**合う** (laugh together — each laughing at the other). **〜合う** (au, to fit together) attached to a verb masu-stem marks a reciprocal action: 助け**合う** (help each other), 見つめ**合う** (gaze at each other), 話し**合う** (discuss together). **〜てしまう** (completion with nuance): 忘れ**てしまった** (completely lost track — ended up forgetting, implying they didn't intend to). **すっかり** reinforces the totality: すっ**かり** 忘れた (completely forgot).

sentences.base_language: The waiter returned to check on them and Akari praised the food with genuine enthusiasm.
sentences.target_language: ウェイターが様子を見に戻ってくると、あかりは心からの言葉で料理を褒めた。
sentences.romanized: Weitā ga yōsu wo mi ni modotte kuru to, Akari wa kokoro kara no kotoba de ryōri wo hometa.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜に来る/戻る purpose**: 様子を見**に戻ってくる** (returns to check on them). **〜に** + motion verb expresses the purpose of movement: 食べ**に行く** (go to eat), 話し**に来る** (come to talk). Here **様子を見に** = for the purpose of checking how things are going. **様子を見る** is a set phrase: to check on something, to see how it's going.
**〜てくると discovery/natural-result**: 戻っ**てくると** あかりは褒めた (when he came back, Akari praised). **〜てくると** combines directional **〜てくる** with the natural-result **〜と** conditional, describing what happened immediately upon his arrival. **心からの言葉** = words from the heart, heartfelt words — **心から** (kokoro kara, from the heart) used as a prenominal phrase with **の**.

sentences.base_language: He smiled warmly and said the chef would be very pleased to hear that.
sentences.target_language: ウェイターは温かく微笑んで、「シェフもきっと喜ぶと思います」と言ってくれた。
sentences.romanized: Weitā wa atatakaku hohoende, 「shefu mo kitto yorokobu to omoimasu」 to itte kureta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜と思う** (I think / believe that ~): 喜ぶ**と思います** (I think [the chef] will be pleased). **〜と思う** introduces an opinion or conjecture, with the plain form before **と**: 行く**と思う** (I think [they] will go), 大丈夫だ**と思います** (I think it's fine). The polite **〜と思います** is the keigo/formal equivalent used in service contexts.
**〜と言ってくれる** (said for one's benefit): **と言ってくれた** (said [that] — doing so as a kindness). **〜てくれる** marks that even verbal acts, not just physical ones, can be benefactive: 励まし**てくれた** (encouraged me — kindly), 教え**てくれた** (told me — helpfully). The 「」brackets mark direct or reported speech in Japanese. **きっと** = surely, certainly (speaker's confident expectation): **きっと** 大丈夫だ (I'm sure it'll be fine).

sentences.base_language: For dessert, Akari chose a matcha parfait with seasonal fruit and red bean paste.
sentences.target_language: デザートに、あかりは旬のフルーツと粒あんをのせた抹茶パフェを選んだ。
sentences.romanized: Dezāto ni, Akari wa shun no furūtsu to tsubu-an wo noseta maccha pafe wo eranda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**のせる** (noseru) = to place on top, to top with — a culinary verb: クリームを**のせる** (top with cream), トッピングを**のせる** (add toppings). As a past prenominal modifier, **のせた** describes the parfait as already topped: クリームを**のせた** ケーキ (a cake topped with cream). The object of **のせた** — **旬のフルーツと粒あんを** — precedes the verb, stacking up before the modified noun **パフェ**.
**〜に (purpose/role)**: デザート**に** 選ぶ (choose as/for dessert). **〜に** marks the role or category into which something is chosen or used: おみやげ**に** 買う (buy as a souvenir), ランチ**に** する (have [it] for lunch). **粒あん** (tsubu-an) = whole red bean paste, where the beans retain their shape — distinguished from **こしあん** (smooth strained paste), a distinction meaningful in Japanese confectionery.

sentences.base_language: When the bill arrived, she was pleasantly surprised by how reasonable the price was for such quality.
sentences.target_language: お会計が来たとき、あかりはこれほどの質でこれほど手頃な値段であることに、嬉しい驚きを感じた。
sentences.romanized: Okaikei ga kita toki, Akari wa kore hodo no shitsu de kore hodo tegoro na nedan de aru koto ni, ureshii odoroki wo kanjita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ことに** (to the fact that ~, as for the fact that ~): 手頃な値段であること**に** 嬉しい驚きを感じた (felt a pleasant surprise at the fact that the price was so reasonable). **〜ことに** nominalizes a clause with **こと** and marks it with **に** as the object or cause of a feeling: 知っていること**に** 驚いた (was surprised at the fact that [they] knew). This is distinct from **〜ことに** as an opener expressing an emotional reaction (嬉しいこと**に** = happily…).
**これほど** repeated for emphasis: **これほどの** 質で **これほど** 手頃な (for quality this high, so reasonable a price). Repeating **これほど** (to this degree) in parallel creates a rhetorical contrast: the quality is high *to this degree*, yet the price is affordable *to this degree*. **手頃** (tegoro) = affordable, moderate, just right — a na-adjective: **手頃な** 価格 (a reasonable price), **手頃な** サイズ (a handy size).

sentences.base_language: They left a warm expression of thanks to the staff before stepping out into the cool night.
sentences.target_language: 涼しい夜の外に出る前に、あかりたちはスタッフに温かいお礼の言葉を伝えた。
sentences.romanized: Suzushii yoru no soto ni deru mae ni, Akari-tachi wa sutaffu ni atatakoi orei no kotoba wo tsutaeta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜前に** (before doing ~): 出る**前に** (before going out). **〜前に** attaches to the plain non-past form of a verb and expresses that the action in the main clause occurs first, before the **〜前に** action: 寝る**前に** 歯を磨く (brush teeth before sleeping), 忘れる**前に** 書いておく (write it down before forgetting). Contrast **〜あとで** (after doing ~).
**伝える** (tsutaeru) = to convey, to communicate, to pass on — used for transmitting feelings, messages, or information: 感謝を**伝える** (convey gratitude), 気持ちを**伝える** (express one's feelings). **お礼の言葉** (orei no kotoba) = words of thanks — **お礼** (orei, gratitude/thanks, with honorific **お**) + **言葉** (kotoba, words). **〜たち** plural suffix: あかり**たち** = Akari and company (those with her).

sentences.base_language: Outside, both women agreed immediately that it had been a truly memorable evening.
sentences.target_language: 外に出ると、二人はすぐに、本当に忘れられない夜だったと言い合った。
sentences.romanized: Soto ni deru to, futari wa sugu ni, hontō ni wasurerarenai yoru datta to ii atta.
literal: 忘れられない夜だったと
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜と natural-result conditional**: 外に出る**と** すぐに言い合った (when they stepped outside, they immediately said to each other). **〜と** here marks an immediate natural consequence — stepping outside triggered the mutual exclamation without delay. This use of **〜と** is distinct from hypothetical **もし〜たら**; it describes something that happened as a natural following event.
**言い合う reciprocal**: **言い合った** (said to each other, agreed mutually). **〜合う** marks reciprocity: 見**合う** (look at each other), 笑い**合う** (laugh together), 言い**合う** (exchange words). **忘れられない** (wasurerarenai) = unforgettable — passive potential negative: **忘れる** → **忘れられる** (can be forgotten / potential passive) → **忘れられない** (cannot be forgotten). This structure is also used for spontaneous emotion: 忘れ**られない** (I just can't forget it — it won't leave me).

sentences.base_language: Akari's feet ached pleasantly — they had strolled through the nearby shrine district before dinner.
sentences.target_language: あかりの足は心地よく疲れていた――夕食の前に、近くの神社の参道をぶらぶらと歩いていたのだ。
sentences.romanized: Akari no ashi wa kokochi yoku tsukarete ita — yūshoku no mae ni, chikaku no jinja no sandō wo burabura to aruite ita no da.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ていた past progressive (explanatory)**: 歩い**ていたのだ** (they had been strolling — here's why). **〜ていた** describes an ongoing action in the past that provides background context. The addition of **のだ** (explanatory particle) signals that this sentence explains or accounts for the preceding statement (the aching feet): it reveals the reason in an 'ah, that explains it' fashion.
**心地よく** (kokochi-yoku) = pleasantly, comfortably — adverbial form of the i-adjective **心地よい** (kokochi-yoi, pleasant, comfortable). **ぶらぶら** is a mimetic adverb for wandering or strolling without a fixed purpose: **ぶらぶら** 歩く (stroll aimlessly), **ぶらぶら** する (wander around, hang around). **参道** (sandō) = the approach path leading to a shrine — a culturally specific term evoking the atmospheric tree-lined or stone-lantern-flanked walkways of Japanese shrines.

sentences.base_language: They decided to take a taxi home rather than walk, feeling too full and content to hurry.
sentences.target_language: お腹もいっぱいで満ち足りた気分だったので、歩かずにタクシーで帰ることにした。
sentences.romanized: Onaka mo ippai de michitarita kibun datta no de, arukazu ni takushī de kaeru koto ni shita.
literal: 満ち足りた気分だったので、
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ことにする** (decide to ~): タクシーで帰る**ことにした** (decided to go home by taxi). **〜ことにする** expresses a deliberate choice made by the subject: 早起きする**ことにした** (decided to get up early), やめる**ことにした** (decided to quit). Contrast **〜ことになった** (it was decided — by external circumstances).
**〜ずに** (without doing ~): 歩か**ずに** タクシーで (by taxi without walking). **〜ずに** is the classical negative te-form, meaning 'without doing ~': 食べ**ずに** 出かけた (went out without eating), 言わ**ずに** 黙った (stayed silent without saying anything). It is equivalent to **〜ないで** but slightly more formal or literary. **満ち足りた** (michitarita) = satisfied, content, fulfilled — past form of **満ち足りる** (to be fully satisfied): **満ち足りた** 表情 (a contented expression).

sentences.base_language: In the taxi, Akari was already looking up the restaurant on her phone, quietly planning her next visit.
sentences.target_language: タクシーの中で、あかりはすでにスマホでレストランを調べながら、次の訪問をひっそりと計画していた。
sentences.romanized: Takushī no naka de, Akari wa sude ni sumaho de resutoran wo shirabete nagara, tsugi no hōmon wo hissori to keikaku shite ita.
literal: レストランを調べながら、
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ながら simultaneous action (two ongoing activities)**: レストランを調べ**ながら** 計画していた (was planning while looking it up). Both actions are simultaneous and both are ongoing (**〜ていた** for the main verb). **〜ながら** with **〜ていた** in the main clause vividly pictures parallel background activities, typical of absorbed or multitasking behaviour.
**〜ていた** narrative state: 計画し**ていた** (was planning — an action in progress at that past moment). **〜ていた** here captures the ongoing, unfinished quality of Akari's planning: she had already begun planning and the process was underway. **ひっそりと** = quietly, unobtrusively, to oneself — a mimetic adverb suggesting something done softly or without drawing attention: **ひっそりと** 涙を流す (shed tears quietly), **ひっそりと** 笑う (smile to oneself). It adds a charming, characterful note — Akari's quiet delight at anticipating the next visit.
